one_line_takeaway: This SoK introduces Security Cube, a 7+3+3(4)-metric evaluation framework (adding attack stability, CIPA, and depth-of-disruption as new formal metrics) and benchmarks 13 jailbreak attacks, 5 defenses, and multiple judges across 10 LLMs, finding reasoning-aligned frontier models (o1-mini, Claude-3.7-Sonnet) cut aggregate ASR to under ~25% while legacy models exceed 90% under adaptive attacks, and that Hidden-State-Guard-style pre-filter defenses and Multi-Agent judges offer the best effectiveness-cost trade-offs among those tested.
key_finding: Legacy pre-2025 models (GPT-3.5-Turbo, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct) show ASRs exceeding 90% under adaptive attacks (e.g., LLM-Adaptive, GPTFuzzer), while the most recent models (o1-mini, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, Qwen-2.5-Max) reduce ASR under the strongest attacks to near zero, with o1-mini and Claude-3.7-Sonnet achieving the lowest aggregate average ASR (~17% and ~23.2% respectively per Table 5, described in text as 'approximately 17%' for the frontier). Per Table 5, per-attack averages across all 10 models: GPT-3.5=59.2%, Qwen=56.6%, Llama=41.6%, Mistral=61.9%, Qwen2.5-Max=35.1%, DeepSeek=61.6%, o1-mini=16.8%, Claude-3-7=23.2%, Gemini-2.0=23.6%, Qwen3-235b=61.8%. By attack (Table 6), ReNeLLM (avg ASR 66.60%, CIPA 0.12), ActorBreaker (61.35%, CIPA 0.11), and LLM-Adaptive (57.65%, CIPA 0.16) are the most effective and broadly generalizable; CodeAttacker and ActorBreaker have the lowest CIPA (0.11), indicating broadest cross-model applicability. Cross-model transfer (Table 7): LLM-Adaptive has the highest average transfer ASR (~43.42%, gamma=0.46); several attacks (PAIR gamma=1.34, Flip gamma=1.32, DrAttack gamma=2.62) transfer MORE effectively to target models than they worked on the source, indicating an asymmetry where prompts crafted against strongly-aligned models bypass weaker targets. Attack overhead (Table 8) spans from CodeAttacker (888.6 tokens, 14.7s) to LLM-Adaptive (444,005 tokens, 667.6s) on Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct. Attack stability (Table 9): LLM-Adaptive is most stable (beta=0.03) due to deterministic white-box generation; ReNeLLM is least stable (beta=0.44) despite high average ASR, showing high-ASR attacks can be unreliable per-prompt. On defense, Hidden State Guard achieves near-zero ASR for 9/11 attacks (Table 10) with only 298.33 tokens and 5904.22 MB overhead (Table 11); Aligner is most resource-intensive (>4K tokens typical, 29.68s latency) and least consistent. For judges (Figure 2), a Multi-Agent judge best matches human labels (delta-ASR=0.34%, F1=0.99, kappa=0.80) but is far more expensive; LlamaGuard offers the best accuracy-cost trade-off, running 47x faster and 18x cheaper in tokens than Multi-Agent while achieving F1=0.90, kappa=0.80.
core_contribution: This is a Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) paper on LLM jailbreak robustness. Its own contributions (distinct from the cited literature it surveys) are: (1) a taxonomy of jailbreak attacks into 7 mechanism-based categories (Logprob-, Shuffle-, LLM-, Multi-round-, Flaw-, Strategy-, and Template-based) and defenses into 5 deployment-stage categories (Pre-filter, System prompt, Fine-tuning, Intra-process, Post-filter); (2) 'Security Cube,' a new multi-dimensional evaluation framework spanning an attacker axis (7 metrics: ASR, attack stability, cross-model transferability, transfer ratio, a newly proposed Concentration Index per Attack (CIPA) inspired by the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index, depth of disruption via hidden-state divergence, and attack overhead), a defender axis (3 metrics: defense success rate, utility preservation, defense overhead), and a judge axis (3-4 metrics: disagreement with humans, Fleiss' kappa inter-annotator agreement, evaluation cost); (3) an original empirical benchmark applying Security Cube to 13 representative attacks, 5 defenses, and multiple automated judges across 10 target LLMs (GPT-3.5-Turbo, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, o1-mini, DeepSeek-v3, Qwen-2.5-Max, Gemini-2.0-Flash, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, Qwen3-235B-A22B); and (4) a 28-work comparison table (Table 3) showing that 20/28 (over 70%) of prior surveys/benchmarks/frameworks rely solely on ASR, motivating the multi-dimensional framework.
threat_model: Red-team adversary against a safety-aligned LLM M_a(.). Adversary goal: craft a jailbreak prompt P_j = T(P_harm) via transformation T that induces the model to produce a response violating its safety policy, judged by a binary function Judge(R, P_harm). Adversary capabilities are split into black-box (query/API access only, can observe refusals and iterate, may use a surrogate model) and white-box (full access to parameters, gradients, architecture, can directly optimize against the target model). Defender capabilities are symmetrically split into black-box (input/output filtering only) and white-box (fine-tuning, internal representation monitoring). Explicit constraints: the paper does NOT assume attacker access to training data, ability to fine-tune the target model, or use of prefilling techniques -- scoping the study to jailbreaks arising from the model's own behavior under normal API-level interaction rather than external manipulation of training or model internals.

datasets_used: HarmBench (primary benchmark dataset for harmful prompts, released early 2024)
quantitative_results: Average ASR by target model (Table 5): GPT-3.5-Turbo 59.2%, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct 56.6%, LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct 41.6%, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 61.9%, Qwen2.5-Max 35.1%, DeepSeek-v3 61.6%, o1-mini 16.8%, Claude-3.7-Sonnet 23.2%, Gemini-2.0-Flash 23.6%, Qwen3-235B-A22B 61.8%

Average ASR and CIPA by attack (Table 6): ReNeLLM 66.60% / CIPA 0.12; ActorBreaker 61.35% / 0.11; LLM-Adaptive 57.65% / 0.16; CodeAttacker 54.57% / 0.11; GPTFuzzer 52.24% / 0.14; PAIR 50.19% / 0.12; PAP 48.58% / 0.12; Flip 41.52% / 0.13; AutoDAN-Turbo 40.17% / 0.12; Multijail 29.74% / 0.14; CipherChat 27.42% / 0.16; DrAttack 25.63% / 0.15; BON 18.13% / 0.16

Cross-model transfer ASR / transfer ratio gamma (Table 7): LLM-Adaptive 43.42% / 0.46; PAP 35.83% / 0.90; ReNeLLM 32.25% / 0.43; ActorBreaker 30.59% / 0.62; AutoDAN-Turbo 30.00% / 0.65; PAIR 28.37% / 1.34; Flip 22.92% / 1.32; Multijail 19.15% / 0.95; CodeAttacker 18.04% / 0.42; DrAttack 11.42% / 2.62; GPTFuzzer 7.84% / 0.31; BON 4.08% / 0.42; CipherChat 3.73% / 0.14
Attack overhead on Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct (Table 8): CodeAttacker 888.6 tokens/14.73s; LLM-Adaptive 444,005 tokens/667.58s (highest cost)
Attack stability beta (Table 9): LLM-Adaptive 0.03 (most stable); ReNeLLM 0.44 (least stable)

Defense success rate highlights (Table 10): Hidden State Guard reaches 100% DSR against most attacks on both Mistral and Llama; SelfReminder is highly variable (e.g., 0.0% on Flip/Mistral vs. 100.0% on Flip/Llama)

Defense overhead (Table 11): CircuitBreaker 0 tokens/0MB/0.21s; SelfReminder 87.01 tokens/0MB/1.36s; Hidden State Guard 298.33 tokens/5904.22MB/3.23s; LlamaGuard 635.69 tokens/15316.51MB/0.82s; Aligner 2278.53 tokens/5904.23MB/29.68s

Judge comparison (Figure 2): Multi-Agent judge delta-ASR=0.34%, F1=0.99, kappa=0.80; LlamaGuard F1=0.90, kappa=0.80, 47x faster and 18x cheaper in tokens than Multi-Agent; Prompt-based Binary Judge delta-ASR=11.66%; LlamaGuard delta-ASR=8.33%; JailJudge delta-ASR=8.00%; human-level kappa=0.84
Cross-survey comparison: 20 of 28 (over 70%) surveyed prior benchmarks/frameworks/surveys evaluate jailbreaks solely via attack success rate (Table 3)
baselines_compared: 13 attacks: LLM-Adaptive, ActorBreaker, BON, Flip, PAIR, AutoDAN-Turbo, ReNeLLM, DrAttack, Multijail, CipherChat, CodeAttacker, PAP, GPTFuzzer
5 defenses: SelfReminder, LlamaGuard, Hidden State Guard, Aligner, CircuitBreaker
Judges: Rule-based, Prompt-based Binary, Prompt-based Score, LlamaGuard, JailJudge, JailbreakEvaluation, Multi-Agent

10 target models: GPT-3.5-Turbo, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, o1-mini, DeepSeek-v3, Qwen-2.5-Max, Gemini-2.0-Flash, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, Qwen3-235B-A22B (plus Gemma-3-27B-it, Qwen3-8B, LLaMA-3.2-3B used only as cross-model transfer targets)
